Part B.key_evidence: The central source is clearly present in the Science image but absent at the same position in the Template, and the Difference image shows a compact positive residual at the center. ndethist=1 and deltajd=0 indicate only a single linked detection so far. The source appears point-like rather than host-centered on extended emission, and the nearest PS1 match is distant (distpsnr1=18.26 arcsec), so catalog morphology is weakly relevant.
Part B.leading_interpretation_and_support: This is most consistent with a real solar-system object (Asteroid benchmark class). The required Science-versus-Template check shows a source in Science with no counterpart at the same central position in Template, producing a clean positive residual. With ndethist=1, deltajd=0, and no obvious persistent host or stellar counterpart at the alert position, a moving object is favored over a fixed astrophysical variable.
Part B.alternative_analysis: A possible alternative is a one-off astrophysical transient such as a supernova, since the subtraction is positive and apparently real. However, the lack of an obvious host at the center, together with the single-detection history and mover-like appearance of a new isolated point source, makes that less convincing than a solar-system interpretation.
